## 3. PROBLEM SECTION

### Section Headline
Your image pipeline shouldn't look like this.

### Section Subheadline
Most developers are doing one of two things. Neither of them is the right answer.

### Pain Points (3 items)

**Pain 1**
- Heading: Manual converters that don't scale
- Description: You paste an image into a browser tab, download the result, and repeat. It works for one image. It breaks the moment your app needs to do it programmatically, in bulk, or on a schedule.

**Pain 2**
- Heading: Enterprise platforms priced for enterprises
- Description: Cloudinary starts at $89/month. Imgix starts at $100/month. They include CDN delivery, video transcoding, and AI tagging — none of which you asked for. You're paying for a platform you're using at 5% capacity.

**Pain 3**
- Heading: Rolling your own Pillow scripts
- Description: You can write a Python script around Pillow. Then you need to add auth, rate limiting, error handling, logging, and deployment. What started as a one-hour task is now a service you're responsible for maintaining forever.

### Transition Line
PixShift is the middle path. A clean, documented, open-source API that does exactly what you need — and nothing you don't.

---

## 4. SOLUTION / FEATURES SECTION

### Section Headline
Everything you need. Nothing you don't.

### Section Subheadline
Five endpoints. Full format support. API keys you control from a dashboard that doesn't require a tutorial.

### Features

**Feature 1**
- Name: Format Conversion
- Benefit headline: Convert any image format with a single POST request
- Description: PNG, JPG, JPEG, WebP, AVIF, GIF, BMP, TIFF as input. PNG, JPG, WebP, or AVIF as output. Send the file, specify the target format, get the converted binary back in the response. No storage, no CDN, no middleman.

**Feature 2**
- Name: Compression
- Benefit headline: Control output file size without writing a line of Pillow code
- Description: Pass a quality value from 1 to 100. The API compresses the image and returns it. Clean HTTP response with the correct Content-Type header. The validation catches bad quality values before Pillow ever sees them.

**Feature 3**
- Name: Resize
- Benefit headline: Resize images with or without aspect ratio lock — your choice
- Description: Set width, height, and whether to lock the aspect ratio. Dimensions cap at 5000px to prevent memory exhaustion. Pass `keep_aspect_ratio=true` and the API handles the math.

**Feature 4**
- Name: API Key Management
- Benefit headline: Create multiple keys, name them, revoke them instantly
- Description: Production key, staging key, a key for that side project — create as many as you need from your dashboard. Each key tracks its own usage. Revoke any of them with one click. Keys are never stored in plain text — only the SHA-256 hash. This is how Stripe does it.

**Feature 5**
- Name: Usage Tracking
- Benefit headline: See exactly how many calls you've made — per key, per hour, per month
- Description: The dashboard shows calls today, this month, and this hour. The `/usage` endpoint exposes the same data programmatically so you can build your own monitoring. No surprise overage bills. No black box.

---

## 5. HOW IT WORKS SECTION

### Section Headline
Up and running in under 60 seconds.

### Section Subheadline
Create an account, get your API key, make your first call. No SDK required — it's just HTTP.

### Steps

**Step 1**
- Label: Create your account
- Description: Register with your email. No credit card. No onboarding call. You're in.

**Step 2**
- Label: Generate an API key
- Description: From your dashboard, create a named key. Copy it — it's shown once, then hashed. Store it in your environment variables.

**Step 3**
- Label: Make your first call
- Description: Send a POST request to `/api/v1/convert` with your file and target format in the `X-API-Key` header. Get the converted image binary back in the response.

**Step 4**
- Label: Ship it
- Description: That's your integration. One endpoint, one header, one response. Add compress or resize as needed. The OpenAPI docs at `/docs` cover everything.

### Closing Line
Your image pipeline is now three lines of curl. The rest is just building your product.

## 7. FAQ SECTION

### Section Headline
Questions we actually get.

**Q: How long does setup take?**
A: About 60 seconds to create an account and get an API key. Your first successful API call will take another 2 minutes if you've used curl before, or 5–10 minutes if you're working from the docs. There's no SDK to install — it's standard HTTP with a header.

**Q: Is this right for me if I'm not building an image-heavy product?**
A: If you need to convert, compress, or resize images in code — even occasionally — PixShift makes sense. The free tier is designed for exactly this: real usage without a subscription forcing you to hit a minimum. You pay (in the future) for what you use, not for platform access.

**Q: I'm currently using Cloudinary. How hard is the migration?**
A: The endpoints are different, but the concept is identical: send a file, get a file back. There's no proprietary SDK to rip out. If you're using Cloudinary purely for format conversion and not for CDN delivery or storage, the migration is a one-afternoon job.

**Q: Is this production-ready or a side project?**
A: The codebase has a full test suite, Alembic database migrations, structured JSON logging, per-key rate limiting, and a custom exception hierarchy with proper HTTP status codes. It is open source — you can read every line and make that judgment yourself. The architecture section of the README covers the design decisions in detail.

**Q: How are API keys stored? Is my key safe?**
A: Your raw API key is shown once at creation and never stored. Only the SHA-256 hash goes into the database. This is the same approach used by Stripe and GitHub. If our database were compromised, an attacker would get hashes — not usable keys. You can verify this in the source code.

**Q: How is this different from just writing my own Pillow script?**
A: You could. The difference is what comes with the API: authentication, rate limiting, usage tracking, error handling, structured logging, database migrations, and a test suite — all already built, already tested, already running. Your Pillow script is a script. PixShift is a service.

## COPYWRITER'S NOTES

**Headline strategy:** Option A leads with the open source + price angle — this is the strongest differentiator and directly addresses the most common objection (why switch from what I have?). Option B leads with the pain, which works best if the audience skews toward developers who are currently using manual converters rather than existing paid APIs. Option C is the cleanest and works well for audiences who already know they want an API and are evaluating options. Recommended test order: A first, then B, with C as the control.

**StoryBrand arc:** The user is a developer with a real, recurring frustration — either they're doing manual conversions (not scalable), paying too much (for features they don't use), or maintaining their own scripts (a hidden time cost). PixShift enters as the guide: precise, open, and boring in the best way. The transformation is simple — their image pipeline goes from a todo item to a solved problem.

**Tension to watch:** The audience is technical, which means they will spot any claim they can't verify. Every quantifiable claim in this copy (100 calls/hour, SHA-256 hashing, five endpoints, formats supported) should match the actual product exactly. If the rate limit changes, the copy must change too.

**For real testimonials:** The most valuable quotes will come from developers who migrated from a paid platform (Cloudinary, Imgix) and can cite a specific cost saving, and from developers who read the source code and trusted it because of what they found there. The open source angle is a trust signal that is rare in this category — testimonials that mention it will outperform generic "great API" quotes.

**Sections to revisit:** The social proof bar and testimonial section need real data before launch. The FAQ answer on production-readiness should be updated once the first production users exist. Pricing is referenced but not specified — once pricing is confirmed, add a pricing section between Features and FAQ.
